title: 3. Challenges in CRTs
text:
Note



Draft scaffold. Each subsection needs a figure (reuse Figs 2 and 5 from the strategy doc), a real-data anecdote from SEARCH / SEARCH-TB / SEARCH-IPT (Balzer et al. 2021), and a forward link to where this site fixes the problem.
This chapter names the three challenges that motivate the methodology developed in Chapters 4–6.

title: 3. Challenges in CRTs
text:
3a. The multilevel-mediation-missing data problem
In CRTs of strategies that aim to expand both reach of an intervention and outcomes among those reached, three features collide:

Hierarchical data — individuals are nested within clusters.
Mediated effect — the intervention’s first action (e.g., screening) mediates its later action (e.g., PrEP uptake).
Missingness on the causal path — underlying eligibility status is observed only among the screened.

Existing approaches that “adjust for differences between screened vs. not screened” block the indirect effect through screening and bias estimates of the overall effectiveness towards the null. This is the problem that motivates the Nakato (2026) ratio-based Stage 1 estimator — see Chapter 6.
Reference: Figure 5 of the strategy doc; Box 1.

title: 3. Challenges in CRTs
section: 3b. Few independent units, small effective sample size
text:
3b. Few independent units, small effective sample size
68% of CRTs randomize fewer than 40 clusters. Within-cluster dependence plus the cluster-level intervention itself shrinks the effective sample size further. Common approaches (mixed models, GEE with finite-sample corrections) tend to perform poorly:



# clusters
t-test bias
GEE+IPW bias
TMLE bias




J = 30
−32%
−2%
−9%



(From simulated CRTs of an average treatment effect of −9% — Table 2 of the strategy doc.) Two-Stage TMLE’s gain comes from (i) Stage-1 within-cluster missing-data adjustment, and (ii) Stage-2 Adaptive Pre-specification of the cluster-level adjustment covariate — see Chapter 4.

title: 3. Challenges in CRTs
text:
3c. Effect heterogeneity
Fewer than 30% of CRTs assess effect heterogeneity well — established subgroups miss data-driven insights, and naïve stratification on a cluster-level modifier (e.g., region) reduces the effective sample to just the clusters with that characteristic. Regression with interaction terms inflates Type-I errors (one simulation: 47.2%).






Note



Year 2 deliverable: a Chapter 7 (or 5b) on data-adaptive identification and evaluation of effect heterogeneity via the cross-validated cluster-level CATE TMLE plus step-down maxT multiple testing — Aim 1B of the R01.

title: 3. Challenges in CRTs
text:
Resources






Tip



Further reading

Strategy doc, Figures 2 and 5; Table 2.
Benitez et al. (2023), Comparative methods for the analysis of cluster randomized trials (Benitez et al. 2023).

title: 6. Hands-on: the multilevel-mediation-missing problem
text:
This chapter walks through the mediated Stage 1 estimator (Nakato and Balzer 2026) on a simulated CRT where:
The cluster-level endpoint is the ratio:
\[
Y^c \;=\; \frac{\mathbb{P}(Y_2 = 1)}{\mathbb{E}\!\bigl[\mathbb{P}(Y_1 = 1 \mid M = 1, W)\bigr]}.
\]
Numerator: empirical proportion uptaking. Denominator: TMLE for the prevalence of risk, fit within each cluster.

title: 6. Hands-on: the multilevel-mediation-missing problem
text:
Generate one CRT
We use a condensed version of the Nakato (2026) DGP. The intervention boosts screening; outcomes only happen among the screened-and-eligible.

library(TwoStageTMLE)
library(data.table)

set.seed(2026)

gen_cluster <- function(N, j) {
  UE1 <- rep(runif(1, -1, 1), N); UE2 <- rep(runif(1, -1, 1), N)
  E1  <- rep(rnorm(1, UE1[1], 0.5), N); E2 <- rep(rnorm(1, UE2[1], 0.5), N)
  W1  <- rnorm(N, 0, 0.5); W2  <- rnorm(N, 0, 0.5); W3 <- rbinom(N, 1, 0.5)
  pY1 <- plogis(0.5 + W1 + W2 - W3 + 0.25*E1 + 0.25*E2)
  data.table(
    id = j, E1 = E1, E2 = E2, W1 = W1, W2 = W2, W3 = W3,
    UE1 = UE1, UE2 = UE2, Y1_star = as.integer(runif(N) < pY1)
  )
}

J <- 20
dat <- rbindlist(lapply(seq_len(J), function(j) gen_cluster(120, j)))
A_per <- sample(c(rep(1L, J/2), rep(0L, J/2)))
dat[, A := A_per[id]]

pM <- plogis(-0.5 + 0.6*dat$A + 0.5*dat$W1 + 0.4*dat$W2 -
              0.4*dat$A*dat$W3 + 0.4*(1-dat$A)*dat$W3 +
              0.1*dat$UE1 + 0.1*dat$UE2)
dat[, M  := as.integer(runif(.N) < pM)]
dat[, Y1 := Y1_star * M]

pY2 <- plogis(0.2 + 0.1*dat$A + dat$W1 + 0.5*dat$W2 +
              2*dat$W3 + 0.2*dat$E1 + 0.2*dat$E2)
dat[, Y2 := ifelse(Y1_star == 1 & M == 1,
                   as.integer(runif(.N) < pY2),
                   0L)]

dat[, c("UE1", "UE2", "Y1_star") := NULL]
head(dat[, .(id, A, W1, W2, W3, E1, E2, M, Y1, Y2)])
#>       id     A          W1          W2    W3         E1        E2     M    Y1
#>    <int> <int>       <num>       <num> <int>      <num>     <num> <int> <int>
#> 1:     1     0 -0.04237439  0.44847287     1 -0.1424984 0.1826801     1     0
#> 2:     1     0 -0.33331981 -0.86939636     1 -0.1424984 0.1826801     0     0
#> 3:     1     0 -1.25804452  0.23442397     0 -0.1424984 0.1826801     0     0
#> 4:     1     0 -0.36757340 -0.27207384     1 -0.1424984 0.1826801     0     0
#> 5:     1     0 -0.51006113 -0.08270708     0 -0.1424984 0.1826801     1     1
#> 6:     1     0  0.05677722  0.27608328     1 -0.1424984 0.1826801     1     0
#>       Y2
#>    <int>
#> 1:     0
#> 2:     0
#> 3:     0
#> 4:     0
#> 5:     1
#> 6:     0

title: 6. Hands-on: the multilevel-mediation-missing problem
section: Stage 2 — cluster-level TMLE on the ratio endpoint
text:
Stage 2 — cluster-level TMLE on the ratio endpoint
Stage 2 is unchanged from Chapter 5 — the ratio endpoint slots into the same machinery:

s2 <- copy(s1)
s2[, Y := tmle]
s2 <- add_weights(s2, target = "clust")

cand <- cand_adj(c("E1", "E2"))

est <- stage2_tmle(
  data          = s2,
  goal          = "RD",
  target        = "clust",
  break_match   = TRUE,
  do_data_adapt = TRUE,
  cand_q_adj    = cand$cand_q_adj, cand_q_form = cand$cand_q_form,
  cand_g_adj    = cand$cand_g_adj, cand_g_form = cand$cand_g_form,
  remove_pscore = TRUE
)
est[, .(est, ci_lo, ci_hi, se, pval, q_adj, g_adj)]
#>            est       ci_lo      ci_hi         se      pval q_adj g_adj
#>          <num>       <num>      <num>      <num>     <num> <int> <int>
#> 1: 0.007673666 -0.04065632 0.05600365 0.02300418 0.7425532     1   -99

title: 6. Hands-on: the multilevel-mediation-missing problem
text:
End-to-end driver

out <- two_stage_tmle_mediated(
  data          = dat,
  ind_cov       = c("W1", "W2", "W3"),
  clust_cov     = c("E1", "E2"),
  sl_library    = c("SL.mean", "SL.glm"),
  goal          = "RD", target = "clust", break_match = TRUE,
  do_data_adapt = TRUE,
  cand_q_adj    = cand$cand_q_adj, cand_q_form = cand$cand_q_form,
  cand_g_adj    = cand$cand_g_adj, cand_g_form = cand$cand_g_form,
  remove_pscore = TRUE
)
out$est        # Stage 2 estimate
out$stage1     # cluster table with all four candidate endpoints

title: 6. Hands-on: the multilevel-mediation-missing problem
text:
A note on variance
Stage 2 computes its variance from the cluster-level influence curve and treats the Stage 1 ratio as a fixed observation at the cluster level. The Stage-1 contribution to variance is asymptotically negligible when within-cluster sample sizes are large. Nakato (2026) verifies acceptable coverage at \(N_c \approx 200\) per cluster, \(J \in \{20, 30, 50, 70\}\).
For inference also robust to the Adaptive Pre-specification selection, pass do_cv_variance = TRUE to stage2_tmle() — the returned data.table gets additional cv_* columns with the CV CI and p-value.

title: 6. Hands-on: the multilevel-mediation-missing problem
text:
Where to go next

The package vignette vignette("mediated-missing") covers the same flow with additional commentary.

Chapter 7 closes with the next steps — Aim 1B (effect heterogeneity), Aim 2 (SAPPHIRE & Peer-led DCP secondary analyses), and the Roadmap Shiny app.

title: 5. Hands-on: missing outcomes
text:
Tip



Live R in the browser. This site uses webR. Chunks marked {webr-r} are editable — change the code, click Run, see the new output. No R install needed. (The cells that call TwoStageTMLE directly still render server-side until the package ships a WebAssembly binary; see the README’s roadmap.)
This chapter walks through the foundational Two-Stage TMLE pipeline (Balzer et al. 2021) on a simulated CRT with missing outcomes. We:

title: 5. Hands-on: missing outcomes
text:
Generate one CRT

library(TwoStageTMLE)
library(data.table)

set.seed(1)
J  <- 30
n  <- c(100, 150, 200)
X  <- TwoStageTMLE:::gen_data_complex(effect = TRUE, n = n, J = J)
O  <- TwoStageTMLE:::get_obs(X, exclude = FALSE)

head(O[, c("id", "pair", "A", "X1", "X2", "M", "Delta", "Y")])
#>   id pair A          X1         X2 M Delta Y
#> 1  1    1 0  0.12171443 -1.1722133 0     0 0
#> 2  1    1 0  0.48678916 -1.0851309 0     0 0
#> 3  1    1 0 -0.19685272  0.5744479 1     1 1
#> 4  1    1 0  0.14024865  0.1330852 1     1 1
#> 5  1    1 0  0.08878864  0.1914228 0     0 0
#> 6  1    1 0 -0.31602077  0.3199612 0     0 0

Delta indicates outcome measurement (0 if missed); Y is NA where Delta = 0. X1, X2, M are individual covariates; X1.c, X2.c are the cluster-level means.






Note



Try it. This cell runs in your browser. Tweak the parameters and see how the cluster-level summaries shift before any TMLE has been applied — useful intuition for why Stage 1 matters.



Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

title: 5. Hands-on: missing outcomes
text:
Stage 2 — cluster-level TMLE + Adaptive Pre-specification

s2 <- copy(stage1)
s2[, Y := tmle]
s2 <- add_weights(s2, target = "clust")

cand <- cand_adj(c("X1.c", "X2.c"))

est <- stage2_tmle(
  data          = s2,
  goal          = "RD",
  target        = "clust",
  break_match   = FALSE,           # preserve the matched pairs
  do_data_adapt = TRUE,
  cand_q_adj    = cand$cand_q_adj, cand_q_form = cand$cand_q_form,
  cand_g_adj    = cand$cand_g_adj, cand_g_form = cand$cand_g_form,
  remove_pscore = TRUE
)
est[, .(est, ci_lo, ci_hi, se, pval, q_adj, g_adj)]
#>            est    ci_lo        ci_hi         se       pval q_adj g_adj
#>          <num>    <num>        <num>      <num>      <num> <int> <int>
#> 1: -0.08921073 -0.17269 -0.005731443 0.03892195 0.03791793     3     1

title: 5. Hands-on: missing outcomes
text:
What APS chose
q_adj and g_adj index the candidate adjustment vector — 1 = "U" (the unadjusted estimator), 2 = "X1.c", 3 = "X2.c". With small clusters and well-balanced randomization, APS often picks the unadjusted estimator for the propensity score (because the trial-arm assignment is known to be 0.5) and the most-predictive covariate for the outcome.

title: 5. Hands-on: missing outcomes
text:
End-to-end driver

out <- two_stage_tmle(
  data       = O,
  ind_cov    = c("X1", "X2", "M"),
  clust_cov  = c("X1.c", "X2.c"),
  goal       = "RD", target = "clust", break_match = FALSE,
  do_data_adapt = TRUE,
  cand_q_adj = cand$cand_q_adj, cand_q_form = cand$cand_q_form,
  cand_g_adj = cand$cand_g_adj, cand_g_form = cand$cand_g_form,
  remove_pscore = TRUE
)
out$est        # the Stage 2 estimate
out$stage1     # the cluster-level table from Stage 1

title: Cluster Randomized Trials: Methods for Reach and Impact
text:
Note



Draft scaffold. This site is under active development. Year-1 content covers Foundations (chapters 1–4) and two hands-on chapters that drive the TwoStageTMLE R package end-to-end. Effect heterogeneity, the Roadmap walker Shiny app, and the SAPPHIRE / Peer-led DCP worked examples will land in Years 2–4.

title: Cluster Randomized Trials: Methods for Reach and Impact
text:
Welcome
Cluster randomized trials (CRTs) randomize groups — communities, clinics, schools, health systems — rather than individuals. They are the design of choice when interventions act at the group level: training community health workers, changing a clinic’s care-delivery model, deploying a public-health campaign. CRTs are increasingly used to evaluate HIV prevention strategies that aim to both reach a target population and improve outcomes among those reached.
Compared with individually-randomized trials, CRTs face three statistical challenges that standard methods handle poorly:

Few independent units. Most CRTs randomize fewer than 40 clusters.
Missing outcomes are nearly universal, and the missingness mechanism is often on the causal pathway (e.g., screening that itself depends on the intervention).
Effect heterogeneity at the cluster-, sub-cluster-, and individual-level is rarely evaluated well — established subgroups miss data-driven insights, and naïve stratification is severely underpowered.

This site presents Two-Stage TMLE (Balzer et al. 2021) — a targeted machine-learning estimator that addresses all three challenges in a single framework. The estimator separates the per-cluster missing-data adjustment (Stage 1) from the between-cluster effect estimation (Stage 2), and is doubly robust, efficient, and preserves Type-I error control with few independent units.

title: Cluster Randomized Trials: Methods for Reach and Impact
text:
What you will learn
By the end of this site, participants will be able to:

State a CRT research question using the Causal Roadmap (Petersen and van der Laan 2014) and identify the multilevel data structure and missing-data mechanism.
Run Two-Stage TMLE on a CRT with missing outcomes using the TwoStageTMLE R package — including Adaptive Pre-specification.
Diagnose when the multilevel-mediation-missing data problem applies (post-baseline screening mediates the effect) and use the Nakato (2026) ratio-based Stage 1 estimator instead.
Write a Statistical Analysis Plan (SAP) for a CRT analysis that pre-specifies the Super Learner library, the candidate adjustment set, and the inference scheme.

title: Cluster Randomized Trials: Methods for Reach and Impact
text:
How to use this site
The chapters are organised in two arcs:

Foundations (chapters 1–3) — conceptual material: why CRTs need special methods, the Causal Roadmap, and the three core challenges.
Two-Stage TMLE (chapters 4–6) — the method itself, plus two hands-on chapters that run real code against simulated CRTs.

Each R-coded chapter is reproducible: code chunks are executable and the data live alongside the chapter. Reading time per chapter: ~20 minutes.

title: Cluster Randomized Trials: Methods for Reach and Impact
text:
Acknowledgements
This work is supported by the National Institutes of Health (PI: Laura Balzer, UC Berkeley). The site is built with Quarto and draws on the design of three earlier sites — the lmtp workshop, the NIH Collaboratory Rethinking Clinical Trials Living Textbook, and the CTML Introduction to Causal Inference.

title: 4. The Two-Stage TMLE method
text:
Note



Draft scaffold. Need: full derivation following §9.3 of the strategy doc + Box 1 / Box 2; redraw Figure 5 inline; add a short video that walks through Stage 1 then Stage 2.

title: 4. The Two-Stage TMLE method
text:
The big idea
Two-Stage TMLE separates missing-data adjustment from between-arm comparison:









Stage
What it does
Independent unit
Estimator




1
Adjust for missing outcomes / mediated missingness within each cluster
Individual
TMLE via ltmle


2
Compare cluster-level endpoints between arms
Cluster
TMLE with Adaptive Pre-specification



The separation is the whole point. Stage 1 handles the messy individual- level data without trying to estimate the effect; Stage 2 sees clean cluster-level data and runs the comparison with maximum efficiency.

title: 4. The Two-Stage TMLE method
text:
Stage 1: within-cluster missing-data adjustment
For each cluster \(c\) separately, fit an individual-level TMLE for the cluster-specific endpoint \(Y^c\). Two variants:

Foundational (missing outcomes)
Used when the outcome itself is differentially measured — e.g., viral suppression status known only for those who returned for follow-up.
\[
Y^c \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\bigl[\mathbb{E}(Y \mid \Delta = 1, W, M)\bigr]
\]
where \(\Delta\) is the outcome-measurement indicator and \((W, M)\) are baseline plus post-baseline covariates. One \(\mathrm{ltmle}\) call per cluster, returning one cluster-level number. See Chapter 5 for hands-on code.


Mediated (the multilevel-mediation-missing problem)
Used when eligibility / risk status is what’s partially missing, mediated by post-baseline screening — e.g., HIV risk status known only for those who got screened.
\[
Y^c \;=\; \frac{\mathbb{P}(Y_2 = 1)}{\mathbb{E}\!\bigl[\mathbb{P}(Y_1 = 1 \mid M = 1, W)\bigr]}
\]
A ratio estimator: numerator empirical, denominator TMLE. See Chapter 6 for hands-on code.

title: 4. The Two-Stage TMLE method
section: Stage 2: cluster-level TMLE with Adaptive Pre-specification
text:
Stage 2: cluster-level TMLE with Adaptive Pre-specification
Hand the \(J\) cluster-level endpoints to a cluster-level TMLE that compares treated to control:
\[
\Psi \;=\; \mathbb{E}\bigl[Y^c(1) - Y^c(0)\bigr]
\]
Adaptive Pre-specification (APS) picks the adjustment variable that minimises the CV variance of the resulting TMLE, while preserving Type-I error control (Balzer et al. 2016). With few clusters, APS routinely yields 4–6× efficiency gains over an unadjusted estimator.

title: 4. The Two-Stage TMLE method
section: Why this is doubly robust and efficient
text:
Why this is doubly robust and efficient
Both stages plug in machine-learning (SuperLearner) candidate fits. Stage 1 is doubly robust in the outcome regression and the \(\Delta\)-mechanism / \(M\)-mechanism. Stage 2 is doubly robust in the cluster-level outcome regression and the (known) randomization distribution.
When both are estimated well, Two-Stage TMLE is efficient — it achieves the smallest asymptotic variance among regular, asymptotically linear estimators.

title: 4. The Two-Stage TMLE method
text:
On variance and finite-sample inference
Stage 2’s variance is computed from the cluster-level influence curve and treats Stage 1’s endpoint as a fixed observation at the cluster level. This is justified asymptotically when within-cluster sample sizes are large; see Box 2 of Balzer et al. (2021). Empirically, Nakato (2026) verifies acceptable coverage at \(N_c \approx 200\) per cluster, \(J \in \{20, 30, 50, 70\}\) — well within the regime of most HIV prevention CRTs.

title: 4. The Two-Stage TMLE method
text:
Where this leaves us
You now know enough to run real code:

Chapter 5 — stage1_tmle() + stage2_tmle() on a CRT with missing outcomes.
Chapter 6 — stage1_mediated() + stage2_tmle() on a CRT with the multilevel-mediation-missing problem.

title: 4. The Two-Stage TMLE method
text:
Resources






Tip

Balzer et al. (2016) — Adaptive Pre-specification.
Nakato and Balzer (2026) — the mediated Stage 1 estimator.

title: Team
text:
Investigators

Laura B. Balzer — PI
Associate Professor of Biostatistics, University of California, Berkeley. Developed Two-Stage TMLE and Adaptive Pre-specification; co-led the SEARCH consortium analyses showing TMLE’s gains over standard CRT methods. 2014 Causality in Statistics Education Award.


Mark van der Laan — Co-Investigator
Professor of Biostatistics, University of California, Berkeley. Inventor of TMLE, Super Learner, and several multiple-testing procedures; author of Targeted Learning (Springer, 2011).


Joshua Schwab — Statistical programmer
Maintainer of the TwoStageTMLE R package.

Contributing

title: 1. Why CRTs?
text:
Note



Draft scaffold. Body needs: (1) ~3 paragraphs framing CRTs vs individually-randomized trials, (2) the HIV prevention motivation (Balzer et al. 2021), (3) brief stats — 700+ active NIH CRTs, 80+ HIV-prevention CRTs, (4) embedded short video (~2 min) from the SAB on why this matters, (5) a “further reading” Resources panel matching the NIH Collaboratory Living Textbook look.

title: 1. Why CRTs?
text:
When you need a CRT
Some interventions act at the group level — village health teams, clinic care-delivery models, public-health campaigns — and cannot be delivered to an individual in isolation. For these, the only valid randomized comparison randomizes groups. Examples:

Universal HIV test-and-treat (SEARCH): 32 communities in Kenya and Uganda randomized to a community-level prevention package vs. standard-of-care.
Dynamic Choice HIV Prevention (SAPPHIRE, Peer-led DCP): 16 and 14 communities respectively, offering choice-based PrEP/PEP delivery.
Isoniazid preventive therapy (SEARCH-IPT): district-randomized trial to improve IPT uptake.

CRTs preserve the rigor of randomization while letting researchers ask questions that map directly to how interventions are deployed in the real world (Balzer et al. 2021). They are increasingly the design of choice for translating efficacy into effectiveness.

title: 1. Why CRTs?
text:
What makes CRTs hard
Compared to individually-randomized trials, CRTs face three persistent challenges:

Few independent units
Most CRTs randomize fewer than 40 clusters. With small numbers of independent units, the dependence among participants within a cluster sharply reduces the effective sample size, and standard inferential tools (mixed models, generalized estimating equations) tend to perform poorly with finite-sample corrections.


Missing outcomes
One review found 93% of CRTs had missing outcomes. Naïve complete-case analyses are biased when participants with measured outcomes differ meaningfully from those with missed outcomes. The problem is worse when the missingness mechanism is itself influenced by the intervention — e.g., when the intervention’s first action is to extend screening.


Effect heterogeneity
Fewer than 30% of CRTs assess effect heterogeneity, because practical guidance on such analyses has been lacking (Balzer et al. 2021). Established subgroups (sex, age group, region) provide some insight, but miss the chance to identify multilevel factors that predict who benefits most.

title: 1. Why CRTs?
text:
Where this site fits
The remaining chapters build a single coherent methodology that addresses all three challenges — anchored on the Causal Roadmap (Chapter 2) and implemented in the TwoStageTMLE R package.

title: 1. Why CRTs?
text:
Resources






Tip



Further reading

David Murray’s NIH ODP online course on pragmatic and group-randomized trials (Murray 2020)
The NIH Collaboratory Rethinking Clinical Trials Living Textbook

title: 2. The Causal Roadmap for CRTs
text:
Note



Draft scaffold. Each Roadmap step below has a one-line placeholder. Year 1 deliverable: fill in steps 1–6 with the SAPPHIRE running example, mirroring Table 6 of the strategy doc. Year 2: embed the Shiny “Roadmap walker” as an iframe at the bottom of this page.

title: 2. The Causal Roadmap for CRTs
text:
The 8-step roadmap
The Causal Roadmap (Petersen and van der Laan 2014) is a step-by-step framework that takes you from a substantive research question to a defensible statistical analysis. Every chapter on this site is keyed to one or more of these steps.

Step 1 — State the research question
The target population, the trial arms, the outcome, and the time horizon.
Running example: “What would be the difference in PrEP initiation within X-weeks if all persons with HIV risk received the intervention vs. the standard-of-care?”


Step 2 — Specify the causal model
A hierarchical (multilevel) causal model that names the variables, their temporal order, and the assumed independencies. For our setup: E^c → W → L → M → Y1* → Y2, with the cluster-level intervention A^c affecting L, M, Y2 directly.


Step 3 — Define the causal effect via counterfactuals
\(\Psi^* = \mathbb{E}^*[Y^c(1) - Y^c(0)]\) where \(Y^c(a^c) = \mathbb{P}^*[Y_2(a^c) = 1 \mid Y_1^*(a^c) = 1]\) — the counterfactual probability of starting PrEP among persons with HIV risk under trial arm \(a^c\).


Step 4 — Specify the observed data
\(O^c = (E^c, A^c, W, L, M, Y_1, Y_2)\) per cluster; one row per individual nested within cluster.


Step 5 — Assess identifiability
Translate the causal estimand into a statistical one — and critically evaluate the assumptions that licence the translation. In our example: PrEP uptake is completely ascertained, and the screened are representative of the unscreened conditional on \((W, L)\).


Step 6 — Specify the statistical estimand
Per [Box 1 of the strategy doc]: at the cluster level, \[
Y^c \;=\; \frac{\mathbb{P}(Y_2 = 1)}{\mathbb{E}\!\bigl[\mathbb{P}(Y_1 = 1 \mid M = 1, W, L)\bigr]}.
\] The trial-level estimand is then \[
\Psi \;=\; \mathbb{E}\bigl[\mathbb{E}(Y^c \mid A^c = 1, E^c) - \mathbb{E}(Y^c \mid A^c = 0, E^c)\bigr].
\]


Step 7 — Statistical estimation and inference
Two-Stage TMLE. See Chapter 4 for the conceptual basis and Chapter 5 for the code.


Step 8 — Interpret
Translate the statistical estimate back into the language of the research question, attending to any identification assumptions that may be challenged.

title: 2. The Causal Roadmap for CRTs
section: Where you are on the roadmap
text:
Where you are on the roadmap
Most of this site lives in Steps 6–7 — the statistical layer. But the guarantees of Two-Stage TMLE depend on the work you do in Steps 1–5, which is why we’ll keep linking back here as we go.

title: 2. The Causal Roadmap for CRTs
text:
Resources






Tip



Further reading

Petersen & van der Laan (2014), Causal Models and Learning from Data (Petersen and van der Laan 2014).
Chapter 1 of Targeted Learning (van der Laan and Rose 2011).

title: 7. Final remarks
text:
Note



Draft scaffold. Final paragraph should pull together the three R01 aims into a coherent dissemination message. Add a “what’s next” call to action and the support-email alias once one exists.

title: 7. Final remarks
text:
What you have now
You have read about — and can run — Two-Stage TMLE in two settings:

Foundational (Chapter 5) — missing outcomes in a CRT.
Mediated (Chapter 6) — the multilevel- mediation-missing data problem when screening sits on the causal path.

Both share the same Stage 2: a cluster-level TMLE with Adaptive Pre-specification, robust and efficient with few independent units.

title: 7. Final remarks
text:
What’s coming

Year 2 — Effect heterogeneity (Aim 1B)
A new chapter on data-driven identification and evaluation of effect modifiers, using cross-validated cluster-level CATE TMLE and the step-down maxT multiple-testing procedure.


Year 2 — The Roadmap Shiny walker
A point-and-click web app, embedded right inside this site, that walks you through Causal Roadmap steps 1–6 and emits a fully-annotated R Markdown analysis file you can knit.


Year 3 — SAPPHIRE worked example (Aim 2)
A real-data worked example on the SAPPHIRE trial — 16 communities, ~80 000 men and women in rural Kenya and Uganda — showing how Two-Stage TMLE refines the primary effectiveness analysis of a dynamic-choice HIV prevention strategy.


Year 4 — Peer-led DCP worked example (Aim 2)
A second real-data worked example on the Peer-led DCP trial — 14 clinic catchment areas, ~560 women working in bars and guesthouses in southwestern Uganda — showing the same methodology in a new implementation context.


Year 5 — Refinements
Final external review board feedback, long-tail FAQ, and an archival snapshot deposited at Zenodo for long-term reproducibility.

title: 7. Final remarks
text:
Get involved
The methods, the code, and this site are all open. Contributions and critiques are welcome:

Code or method issue? Open a ticket at https://github.com/LauraBalzer/TwoStageTMLE/issues.
Suggestion for the site? Open a PR or an issue at the site’s repo.
Question that doesn’t fit either? [Support email — TBD].

title: 7. Final remarks
text:
Citation
If you use the methods or code in your work, please cite:

Balzer L.B., van der Laan M., Ayieko J., Kamya M., Chamie G., Schwab J., Havlir D., Petersen M. (2021). Two-Stage TMLE to Reduce Bias and Improve Efficiency in Cluster Randomized Trials. arXiv:2106.15737.

For the mediated Stage 1 estimator:

Nakato J., Balzer L.B. (2026). Methods for evaluating overall effectiveness of cluster-level strategies aiming to improve reach and health outcomes. [arXiv: forthcoming.]

title: 7. Final remarks
